payload.claim_text: C line schedule updates produced a 9.0-point OTP improvement from October and the line's best non-COVID monthly performance.
payload.description: Schedule update on C line improved OTP by 9.0 percentage points

records.raw_text: Similar updates on the C produced a 9.0-point improvement from October, and the line's best non-COVID monthly performance.

payload.claim_text: The B's new schedule improved resilience to incidents and helped trains stay on time, significantly reducing customer wait times in November 2025.
payload.description: Schedule update on B line improved incident resilience and on-time performance

records.raw_text: The B 's new schedule improved our resilience to incidents and helped trains stay on time across the line, significantly reducing customer wait times across November.

payload.claim_text: Every station and every subway car is already equipped with security cameras.
payload.description: Full security camera coverage across all subway stations and cars

records.raw_text: On Monday, December 8th, we implemented the first permanent change to the subway network since 2017 by swapping the routes the F and M take between Queens and Manhattan during the week.

The MTA is continuing to make the subway system accessible for all faster than ever before. On November 25, 2025, the MTA celebrated the completion of station and accessibility upgrades at the 118-year-old Church Av Station in Brooklyn, including opening a new entrance mid-block on East 18th St.

description: A bus-mounted camera system that issues violations to vehicles occupying bus lanes, to double parked vehicles along bus routes, and to vehicles blocking bus stops. Administered in partnership between the MTA, NYC Department of Finance (DOF), and NYC Department of Transportation (DOT). The 2023 NYS Legislature passed a provision in the budget to expand ACE. Currently active on 60 bus routes.

ACE will be implemented in May 2024 as a four-year demonstration. Activated on fourteen routes in May 2024, expand to another 400 buses and fourteen additional routes, then add ten routes per year from 2025 through 2027
Expansion of ACE program beyond bus lanes to vehicles blocking bus stops and double-parked vehicles. Cameras added to up to 1,000 buses and enforced on 28 bus routes.
Expanded scope of ABLE program to also monitor and ticket vehicles obstructing bus stops and double-parked vehicles, following legislative approval.
Expanded scope of ABLE program to include monitoring and ticketing vehicles obstructing bus stops as well as double-parked vehicles, following legislative approval

Expansion of the Automated Bus Lane Enforcement Program (ABLE), renamed ACE. Issues tickets to cars illegally parked in bus stops and bus lanes. Launched June 2024 on 14 existing ABLE routes with state-of-the-art enforcement technology. NYS Budget approved expansion to enforce bus lanes, bus stops, and illegal double parking during a four-year demonstration period.

Activated ACE on 34 routes in partnership with NYC DOT and NYC DOF. As of November 29, all routes transitioned from warning period to issuing violations. Bus lane speeds increased on average by 5%, 20% reduction in collisions, 5-10% estimated reduction in emissions. Covers 1,000 buses.

Expansion of Automated Camera Enforcement (ACE) adding 10 new bus routes on September 16, 2024 and another 10 on September 30, 2024. Total reaching 34 routes and over 1,000 ACE-equipped buses across all five boroughs.
Program issuing tickets for double parking, blocking bus stops, and driving in bus lanes via bus-mounted cameras
expand ACE to 30 additional routes and 1,000 more buses, beginning with the M2 and M4 at the end of April
Covers 39 routes with over 1,200 equipped buses serving 775,000 daily riders; includes bus lane, bus stop, and double-parking enforcement

Modification 2 to Hayden for bus stop and double-parked vehicle enforcement and up to 1,000 additional ACE systems; Modification 1 to Seon for bus stop, double-parked, bike lane, and paratransit stop enforcement on up to 1,000 ACE systems

Started as Automated Bus Lane Enforcement (ABLE) in 2019, evolved into ACE. Uses cameras on buses to enforce bus lanes, bus stops, and double-parking rules. Active on 58 routes, covering about 560 miles of streets across all five boroughs.
Automated Camera Enforcement system for bus lanes, bus stops, double-parked vehicles, bike lanes, and paratransit stops
status: 60-day warning period started September 16, 2024 on 10 new bus routes; additional 10 routes starting September 30, 2024

payload.description: A bus-mounted camera system that issues violations to vehicles occupying bus lanes, to double parked vehicles along bus routes, and to vehicles blocking bus stops. Administered in partnership between the MTA, NYC Department of Finance (DOF), and NYC Department of Transportation (DOT). The 2023 NYS Legislature passed a provision in the budget to expand ACE. Currently active on 60 bus routes.

records.raw_text: Since its launch in June 2024, the program has extended across all five boroughs, equipping more than 1,400 buses and covering 560 miles of service, benefitting over 915,000 daily customers.

payload.description: Network of 24/7 staffed Customer Service Centers for fare payment support and travel information

records.raw_text: NYCT launched a network of Customer Service Centers (CSCs) beginning in 2023. The first 15 CSCs opened between 2022 and 2024. In 2025, NYCT has opened an additional six locations with nine more to open by the end of the year.

description: First-of-its-kind full-scale subway car facility, first brand new facility on new property added to the network in 77 years
Design and construction of a new facility to test and accept rail cars for NYCT, including two indoor tracks each accommodating five railcars. Contract awarded to JTCM Partners for $91,900,000.

Design and construction of new facility to test and accept rail cars for NYCT in Brooklyn. Facility to include two indoor tracks, each accommodating five railcars at a time, designed to ensure operational efficiency with all features, systems and equipment needed to support railcar receiving and testing operation.
First-of-its-kind facility to test and process more than 1,500 new subway cars, delivered on time and $5.5 million under budget

payload.description: First-of-its-kind full-scale subway car facility, first brand new facility on new property added to the network in 77 years

extra_fields.design_builder: JTCM Partners (J-Track LLC, TC Electric LLC, MLJ Contracting Corp.)

records.raw_text: Transit celebrated the opening of the first-of-its-kind Railcar Acceptance and Testing Facility. The facility, located in Sunset Park, Brooklyn, can accept new cars by truck, rail, and barge, and even has access to the mainline tracks. Every new car Transit receives will come through this place, and we expect to process 30 new railcars a month here.
